context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Peer Assessment (Power/Actor Maps)

Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST for initial submission, plus one additional week for peer review.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week.

Assignment Tasks
Following the completion of your Power/Actors Map, you will continue to complete your Peer Assessment Review of two of your fellow learners' Power/Actor Maps from the ORA assignments in Week 4. Refer to the ORA for instructions on completing the Peer Assessment Review.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk tbh I’m kinda lost on this peer assessment thing for the power/actor maps. Can u just explain the whole thing from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — it can feel overwhelming at first! Before I jump in, tell me: have you already completed your own Power/Actor Map? That's the starting point for this peer review, so knowing where you stand will help me help you better.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole assignment for them, which would be doing the work for them. Instead, I should find out what they already know and where exactly they are stuck, using a Socratic approach to help them identify their own gaps. I'll ask a targeted question to narrow down the confusion rather than giving a full explanation.

exchanges.student: uh maybe? like I did some of it but now I’m confused about the review part too, fr. Can u just walk me thru all of it rn?
exchanges.tutor: Let's take it one step at a time — I won't walk you through everything at once, but I can definitely help you get unstuck. First things first: what does your Power/Actor Map currently include? What have you mapped out so far for your case study city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to walk them through the whole assignment, which I should not do. I need to set clear boundaries and focus on one thing at a time. I should first address the incomplete Power/Actor Map before moving to the peer review, since the assignment requires completing the map first. I'll ask a specific question to understand where they are with their own map.

exchanges.student: ngl I’m not even sure what counts, just some actors and arrows I think. maybe city gov, residents, businesses, idk.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — those are reasonable actors. Now think about this: what do the arrows in your map actually represent? What are they trying to show about the relationships between those actors?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified some actors but seems uncertain about whether they are sufficient. Rather than validating or critiquing their list directly, I should use a Socratic question to help them think about what makes a Power/Actor Map meaningful in the context of climate action. This will help them self-evaluate their own work before moving to the peer review.

exchanges.student: that’s the part messing me up tbh, like power maybe? influence? I can’t tell if I’m supposed to explain all that in the peer review too.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct — power and influence are both relevant. Here's a question to help you think it through: in the context of climate action in your city, who has the ability to make decisions or block decisions? Does your map currently show that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is conflating two things: understanding their own map and understanding the peer review. I should help them separate these two issues. First, let me help them clarify what arrows represent in their own map, since that understanding is foundational to being able to review someone else's map well. A targeted Socratic question will help them think it through.

exchanges.student: maybe the city gov? but then utilities or developers too? idk, this is why I just want the full overview bc I’m confused on the map and the review part now.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually identifying the right kinds of actors! City gov, utilities, developers — those are meaningful players in climate decisions. Rather than a full overview, here's what I'd suggest: take what you've told me and write it down as a rough description of your map. What actors do you have, and what does each arrow mean? Once you can describe it clearly, the peer review will make a lot more sense.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress on identifying actors with decision-making power but keeps looping back to wanting a full overview. I should acknowledge their progress, gently push back on the request for a full walkthrough, and help them consolidate what they have so far before moving to the peer review. I'll ask them to think about what they have and prompt them to write something down, since we've been going in circles a bit on the map.

exchanges.student: I still don’t really get what the peer review wants tho, like what am I even looking for in other ppl’s maps? Can u explain that part from scratch?
exchanges.tutor: The ORA has the specific peer review instructions — have you checked those yet? And think about it this way: based on what you now know a good Power/Actor Map should include, what kinds of things do you think you'd be looking for in someone else's?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now shifting focus to the peer review before consolidating their own map understanding. They're asking me to explain the peer review from scratch, which I shouldn't do. The assignment states the ORA has instructions for the peer review, so I should point them there while also asking a Socratic question to help them think about what reviewing someone else's map might involve based on what they already know about their own map.

exchanges.student: I looked but it still feels super vague tbh. I guess I’m supposed to see if their actors make sense maybe, but I’m still lost on the overall point.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid instinct. Now push that further — what would make an actor 'make sense' in a climate action context? What would you expect to see explained about each actor in a well-done map?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress - they identified that checking if actors make sense is part of the review. I should affirm this and use a Socratic question to help them think deeper about what 'makes sense' means in context. I want to help them build their own framework for evaluation rather than giving them a checklist.

exchanges.student: idk maybe if they matter to climate stuff and have some kind of influence? but I’m still not seeing the big picture, can u pls just summarize the whole assignment really quick.
exchanges.tutor: I won't summarize the assignment for you — that's not my role here. But notice what you just said: actors should matter to climate issues AND show some kind of influence. That's actually meaningful criteria! You've built that yourself over our conversation. At this point, I'd like you to try writing 2-3 sentences describing what you think makes a strong Power/Actor Map. Can you do that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a full summary/overview, which I should not provide. They are also making genuine progress - their answer about actors mattering to climate and having influence is good. I've noticed a pattern of the student trying to get me to explain everything rather than building understanding themselves. I should acknowledge their good answer, set a clear boundary about summarizing the assignment, and push them to integrate what they've learned so far into something written.

exchanges.student: ugh I can try but I feel like I’m guessing, fr. maybe a strong map has relevant actors, shows who influences who, and makes the relationships clear?
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid description — relevant actors, influence relationships, and clarity. Now here's the connection: if that's what makes YOUR map strong, what does that tell you about what to look for when reviewing someone ELSE's map?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely good answer that captures key elements of a strong Power/Actor Map. I should affirm this clearly since they worked to get here, and then bridge this to the peer review task, which is the next step. This is a good moment to help them see how their own understanding of a good map directly translates into peer review criteria.

exchanges.student: so like… if their actors fit the city’s climate issue, if the arrows/links show influence clearly, and if the map is easy to follow? I think that’s it but I’m still kinda unsure.
exchanges.tutor: You just answered your own question! That's a legitimate framework for a peer review. Now go back to the ORA instructions and check if there are any additional specific criteria listed — your framework might be missing something the instructors specifically asked for. You're in a much better place than when we started.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has essentially derived their own peer review framework through guided discovery. This is excellent progress. I should affirm this strongly and point out that they've answered their own question. Since we're near the end of our planned exchanges, I should encourage them to now go apply this to the actual peer review using the ORA instructions, and remind them to check those instructions for any additional specific criteria.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated strong Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently refusing to summarize or walk through the assignment directly, instead guiding the student to build their own understanding incrementally. No near-submission-ready content was provided, key ideas were student-generated, and the tutor built on each student response effectively. Responses were appropriately concise and the tone was supportive without being evaluative.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor never gave direct answers or submission-ready content; every substantive insight (relevant actors, influence relationships, clarity) was elicited from the student through targeted questions. The tutor acknowledged and built on student progress at each turn (e.g., 'You're actually identifying the right kinds of actors,' 'That's actually a solid description'), satisfying 1.2.A. The tutor provided process-level feedback in Turn 8 by explicitly naming the criteria the student had constructed themselves, satisfying 1.3. Dialogue remained on-task throughout with no off-topic drift, and the tutor redirected the student's repeated requests for a full overview without engaging with them substantively. Responses were bite-sized and conversational, and tone was consistently warm and encouraging without implying formal grading.
